text: Superior mesenteric artery syndrome, also known as SMA syndrome, is a vascular compression disorder where SMA causes duodenal compression and outlet obstruction. We present a forty-eight year old woman with a two year history of intermittent nausea and vomiting, despite being on intermittent TPN for several months and a twenty pound weight gain. Her workup included multiple studies, including endoscopies. On upper GI, we are able to see that the stomach is quite pateous and extends all the way down into our pelvis. Here is another example of a coronal section on the CT scan demonstrating the enlarged stomach as well as the proximal obstruction of the duodenum. Here on the CT angio, we are able to demonstrate an aorta mesenteric angle, which is normally twenty-five to sixty degrees, but here we see approximately eleven degree angulation.

After placing the trochars, the surgeon examines the patulous and dilated stomach, along with the first and beginning second portion of the duodenum, which appear dilated due to chronic obstruction from SMA syndrome. The transverse colon is lifted to reveal the ligament of Treitz. The superior mesenteric artery vessels are visible, and the third and fourth portions of the duodenum are decompressed.

The surgeon exposes the C-loop of the duodenum using a harmonic scalpel and sharp dissection, preparing for the Coker maneuver. The transverse colon is lifted to reveal the dilated portion of the duodenum beneath the C-loop. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels are visible, causing vascular compression and outlet obstruction of the duodenum. The jejunum, approximately twenty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz, is selected and positioned as an anticholic limb.

The surgeon places a stay suture in the selected portion of the jejunum and attaches it to the C-loop of the duodenum. A posterior row of running 3-0 sutures is hand-sewn. An enterotomy is created on both the small bowel and duodenal side for a planned side-to-side anastomosis, measuring five centimeters in total. The inner layer is then sewn using running micro sutures.

The surgeon completes the anterior inner layer of the anastomosis using running 3-0 vacuo-lumbar sutures, finalizing the two-layered hand-sewn side-to-side anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum. An endoscopy is performed, visually confirming the wide-open anastomosis.

Postoperatively, an upper GI was then obtained, showing contrast flowing freely through the anastomosis into the jejunum. After resolution of her delayed gastric emptying, the patient was discharged on post-operative day four and advanced to a regular diet.
